metadata.title: Causal Inference — Counterfactual Confound Test
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Ablation test: seasonal features vs operational signal.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Your supply chain disruption model has an eighty percent confidence rate — and you trust it. But what if most of those high-confidence predictions are driven by the month of the year, not actual stress signals? A counterfactual confound test answers that question: ablate the seasonal features and watch which predictions survive. The ones that collapse were never real.
beats.visual_intent: title card: high-confidence model vs ablated model — how many predictions survive without seasonal features; the distinction between seasonal confounding and genuine operational stress

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your supply chain disruption model has an eighty percent confidence rate — and you trust it. But what if most of those high-confidence predictions are driven by

beats.narration_text: Into the terminal: claude — build a Python counterfactual confound test for a supply chain disruption model: generate seven hundred thirty days synthetic data with seasonal confounding; train a calibrated logistic regression; ablate month, quarter, and seasonal features; count high-confidence predictions that survive versus collapse.

Read it. The ablation logic is one comparison: baseline probability above eighty percent, and ablated probability still above eighty percent — that prediction survived. The count of collapses is the count of predictions that needed seasonal features to be confident.

props.code:
# confound_test.py — Claude Code output
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV
import numpy as np

# ablate seasonal features: month, quarter, seasonal_index
ablated_X = X.drop(columns=['month','quarter','seasonal_index'])

baseline  = clf.predict_proba(X)[:, 1]
ablated   = clf.predict_proba(ablated_X)[:, 1]

# surviving = high-confidence predictions that hold WITHOUT seasonal
surviving = sum(1 for p_base, p_ablate in zip(baseline, ablated)
                if p_base >= 0.8 and p_ablate >= 0.8)

beats.narration_text: Run it. The timeline shows twelve high-confidence predictions. After ablating seasonal features, eight dissolve — they were seasonal confounds. Four survive — those are operational signals. Sixty-seven percent of the model's confidence was seasonal noise.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: animated timeline — 12 flags appear (8 CRIMSON seasonal, 4 PASS_CLR genuine), then 8 seasonal flags dissolve, 4 genuine remain; counter updates from 12 to 4 survivors

beats.narration_text: Four survivors out of twelve is useful — but check whether those four are geographically clustered. If they are, there is a regional confounder we haven't ablated. Iterate: add supplier-region as an ablation target and re-run.

props.command:
claude "update confound_test.py:
  -> also ablate supplier_region feature
  -> show how many of the 4 survivors collapse
  -> report final genuine signal count"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. Two of the four survivors collapse under regional ablation — they were regional confounds, not operational stress. Two predictions survive both ablations: those are the causal signals.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal output showing 2 survivors after regional ablation, 2 genuine causal signals identified

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Re-run. Two of the four survivors collapse under regional ablation — they were regional confounds, not operational stress. Two predictions survive both ablation

beats.narration_text: Twelve confident predictions. Eight seasonal confounds. Two regional confounds. Two genuine signals. The ablation test is a counterfactual argument: if the prediction cannot survive without the confounding feature, it never had causal standing to begin with.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 12 predictions -> ablate seasonal -> 4 remain -> ablate regional -> 2 genuine; the counterfactual argument stated plainly

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Twelve confident predictions. Eight seasonal confounds. Two regional confounds. Two genuine signals. The ablation test is a counterfactual argument: if the pred

beats.narration_text: Your move: run this on your own model. Rank your features by how often they appear in collapsing versus surviving predictions — that ranking is a causal plausibility score. Any high-confidence prediction that cannot survive feature ablation is a liability, not an asset.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run ablation on your own model, 2) rank features by survival rate, 3) treat non-survivors as liabilities
